Northern royal albatross: The Taiaroa Head colony is the only albatross colony found on a human-inhabited mainland in the Southern Hemisphere.
Southern royal albatross: The southern royal albatross has an average lifespan of 58 years.
Dalmatian pelican: Edward Lear, the renowned 19th-century artist and poet best known for his nonsense literature, created the first widely known illustration of the Dalmatian pelican as a plate in John Gould's *The Birds of Europe*.
Andean condor: The female Andean condor is smaller than the male, which is an exception to the usual sexual dimorphism seen in birds of prey.
Amsterdam albatross: The Amsterdam albatross chick weighs more than its parents at the peak of weight gain before losing weight as it grows feathers.
Antipodean albatross: Unlike other albatross species, Antipodean albatrosses are not recorded eating any crustaceans, feeding predominantly on cephalopods and to a lesser extent fish.
Great white pelican: The male great white pelican can average more than 30% more massive than the female, making it one of the species with the most pronounced sexual dimorphism among pelicans.
Himalayan vulture: In a large party, Himalayan vultures can strip a carcass of all tissue in 30 minutes, and do the same to a yak carcass in roughly 120 minutes.
California condor: The California condor can live up to 60 years, making it one of the world's longest-living birds.
Grey heron: When George Neville became Archbishop of York in 1465, 400 herons were served to the guests.
African marsh harrier: Unlike many other harriers, the African marsh harrier is monogamous and remains on its breeding territory for most of the year.
Large-billed crow: In Japan, large-billed crows are known for their regular, unprovoked attacks on humans, especially when passing through near their nesting areas.
Ural owl: The Ural owl has one of the longest tails of any owl species, with only the great grey owl certainly having a longer tail among extant owls.
Pink-footed shearwater: Pink-footed shearwaters perform trans-equatorial migrations of 11,000 km, with some individuals travelling up to 31,000 km in total from their breeding sites in Chile.
Australian shelduck: Young Australian shelducks often aggregate in crèches of 20-40 birds, and even up to 100 birds rarely.
White-eyed gull: The white-eyed gull's eye is not actually white; instead, it has white eye-crescents and takes its name from these markings around the eye.
Sulawesi serpent eagle: The Sulawesi serpent eagle has a wingspan of 105-120 cm despite measuring only 46-54 cm in length.
Pale chanting goshawk: Pale chanting goshawks commonly associate with foraging honey badgers and feed on organisms that are flushed by the honey badger's feeding activity, while the honey badger does not suffer any loss of foraging efficiency.
Philippine serpent eagle: A female Philippine serpent eagle was collected with a single egg in its oviducts in April of 2020.
Streaked shearwater: A streaked shearwater specimen has been recorded in Wyoming, far inland from its typical Pacific Ocean habitat.
Lappet-faced vulture: Lappet-faced vultures are believed to occasionally attack live animals and have been recorded predating on flamingo colonies, young impalas, and guineafowl nests.
American white pelican: The American white pelican can live for more than 34 years in captivity.
Bearded vulture: The bearded vulture's stomach acid has a pH of around 1 and can digest large bones in about 24 hours.
Eurasian griffon vulture: The maximum recorded lifespan of a griffon vulture in captivity is 41.4 years.
Marabou stork: Marabou storks have been observed devouring shoes and pieces of metal when feeding on human garbage.
Cape vulture: The Cape vulture is significantly heavier than the lappet-faced vulture despite the latter's extremely large appearance, with a mean body mass of roughly 8.9 to 9.22 kg compared to wild lappet-faced vultures.
Saddle-billed stork: A saddle-billed stork killed and consumed a spitting cobra.
Cinereous vulture: The cinereous vulture has genetic variations that allow it to digest carcasses and resist pathogens better than bald eagles, as revealed by a 2015 genome sequencing study.
Wattled crane: The wattled crane has the smallest average clutch size of any crane species in the world, with an average of only 1.6 eggs.
Jabiru: The jabiru is the tallest flying bird found in South America and Central America, often standing nearly the same height as the flightless greater rhea.
Trumpeter swan: The trumpeter swan is one of the heaviest flying birds in the world, weighing 7–13.6 kg (15–30 lb) on average.
Australian pelican: The Australian pelican has been recorded as having the longest bill of any living bird, with the record-sized bill measuring 50 cm (20 in) long.
White-rumped vulture: During the 1980s, the white-rumped vulture was thought to be "the most abundant large bird of prey in the world," but by 2021 the global population had declined to fewer than 6,000 mature individuals.
Rüppell's vulture: A Rüppell's vulture was confirmed to have flown into an airplane engine at an altitude of 11,300 meters (37,000 feet) over Ivory Coast in 1973.
Red-crowned crane: The maximum known weight of the red-crowned crane is 15 kg (33 lb 1 oz), which is heavier than the average adult human brain.
Great bustard: The heaviest verified great bustard specimen, collected in Manchuria, weighed about 21 kg (46 lb), making it the world record for heaviest flying bird.
Siberian crane: In captivity, one individual Siberian crane was recorded to have lived for nearly 62 years while another lived for 83 years.
Shy albatross: Some shy albatross are able to fly 1,000 kilometres in 24 hours.
Spot-billed pelican: Fishermen in eastern Bengal once used spot-billed pelicans as decoys because they believed an oily secretion from the birds attracted certain fish species.
Magnificent frigatebird: The female magnificent frigatebird stays to provision for the young for almost a year after the male abandons the chick.
Campbell albatross: The Campbell albatross can live to well beyond 50 years, though the average life expectancy is given as only 28 years due to lack of study.
Bald eagle: Bald eagles build nests up to 4 meters (13 feet) deep, 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) wide, and weighing 1 metric ton, which are the largest tree nests ever recorded for any animal species.
Mute swan: One unusually big Polish cob weighed almost 23 kg (51 lb), which counts as the largest weight ever verified for a flying bird.
Whooper swan: The whooper swan has been the national bird of Finland since 1981 and is featured on the Finnish 1 euro coin.
Red-headed vulture: The red-headed vulture population has essentially halved every other year since the late 1990s due to diclofenac, causing a species that once numbered in the hundreds of thousands to come dangerously close to extinction in less than two decades.
White-headed vulture: White-headed vultures show reversed sexual dimorphism, with females being larger than males, making them unique among African vultures.
Slender-billed vulture: Diclofenac, a common pain-relief drug used on livestock, is poisonous to vultures and causes kidney failure, making it the primary driver of the species' catastrophic 97% population decline.
Indian vulture: Just 0.8% of livestock carcasses containing diclofenac can cause a significant crash in vulture populations.
Peruvian pelican: Peruvian pelicans occasionally cannibalize unrelated chicks of their own species.
Short-tailed albatross: The short-tailed albatross was hunted so intensively for its feathers in the late 19th century that an estimated 10 million birds were destroyed.
Common crane: The cranberry is possibly named after the common crane species.
Goliath heron: The Goliath heron can swallow fish weighing up to 1.4 kg (3.1 lb), which is nearly one-third of its own body weight.
Steller's sea eagle: A wildlife veterinarian was badly injured when a female Steller's sea eagle grabbed his arm and embedded her talons, piercing through to the other side of his arm.
White-backed vulture: A 2024 study showed that the white-backed vulture population had fallen by 86%.
Black-browed albatross: A black-browed albatross known as Albert was blown off course into the North Atlantic in 1967 and has been sighted in Scottish gannet colonies for years, spending summers in north Scotland.
Great frigatebird: Male great frigatebirds can inflate a bright red gular sac like a balloon over 20 minutes to attract mates.
Grey-headed albatross: They are capable of diving as deep as 7 meters to chase prey, but do not do so frequently.
White-tailed eagle: A sea eagle population lived in the Hawaiian Islands for over 100,000 years as the largest terrestrial predator there, but went extinct for unknown reasons.
Christmas frigatebird: The male Christmas frigatebird inflates a striking red gular sac to attract a mate.
Brown pelican: In 1903, Theodore Roosevelt set aside the first National Wildlife Refuge, Florida's Pelican Island, specifically to protect the brown pelican from hunters.
Whooping crane: By 1941, the whooping crane population had been reduced to just 21 wild birds and 2 captive birds.
Brolga: The brolga is the heaviest flying bird regularly found in mainland Australia, averaging slightly heavier than black swans and Australian pelicans.
Sandhill crane: Sandhill cranes have one of the longest fossil histories of any extant bird, with the oldest unequivocal sandhill crane fossil being 2.5 million years old.
Buller's albatross: Buller's albatrosses produce a stomach oil made up of wax esters and triglycerides that they use both as a defense against predators and as an energy-rich food source for chicks and themselves during long flights.
Light-mantled albatross: Light-mantled albatrosses are capable of breeding until at least 32 years old and living to 40 or longer.
Martial eagle: Female martial eagles can weigh nearly three times as much as male martial eagles, with females ranging from 4.45 to 6.5 kg while males range from 2.2 to 3.8 kg.
Wedge-tailed eagle: Wedge-tailed eagles have rebounded to similar or even higher numbers than before European colonisation, thanks in part to humans inadvertently providing food sources such as rabbits and roadkill.
Black-footed albatross: An incubating albatross has been recorded going without food or water for 49 days while waiting to be relieved by its mate.
Greater yellow-headed vulture: The greater yellow-headed vulture lacks a syrinx and can therefore only make grunts or low hisses, unable to produce any other vocalizations.
Laysan albatross: A female Laysan albatross named Wisdom laid her estimated 60th egg in December 2024 at an estimated age of 74 years old.
Black-necked stork: The black-necked stork and the saddle-billed stork are the only stork species that show marked sexual dimorphism in iris colour, with females having yellow irises and males having dark-coloured irises.
Golden eagle: Golden eagles maintain home ranges or territories that may be as large as 200 km² (77 sq mi).
Steppe eagle: The steppe eagle is the only eagle to nest primarily on the ground.
Secretarybird: The secretarybird's toes are only about 20% of the length of those of an eagle of the same size, making it unable to grasp objects with its feet.
King vulture: King vultures have been known to live for up to 30 years in captivity.
Australasian gannet: Sir Joseph Banks shot three Australasian gannets in New Zealand waters on Christmas Eve 1769 and they were cooked in a goose pie for the sailors' Christmas dinner the next day.
Verreaux's eagle: Verreaux's eagle has been continuously studied in the Matobo Hills of Zimbabwe since the late 1950s, making it arguably the best studied eagle population in the world.
Sooty albatross: Sooty albatrosses mate for life and return to the exact same breeding location every other year.
Ascension frigatebird: Male Ascension frigatebirds have a striking red gular sac which they inflate to attract a mate.
Spanish imperial eagle: In one case, an adult Spanish imperial eagle killed a cinereous vulture, the largest accipitrid in the world, while protecting its own nest.
Turkey vulture: The turkey vulture's nostrils are not divided by a septum, but rather are perforate, meaning one can see through the beak from one side to the other.
Tundra swan: Scientists make detailed drawings of each Bewick's swan's bill pattern and assign names to individual swans because every bird's bill pattern is unique.
White-bellied sea eagle: The white-bellied sea eagle and Sanford's sea eagle, despite differing greatly in appearance and ecology, have a genetic divergence of only 0.3%, suggesting their ancestors might have diverged as recently as 150,000 years ago.
Eastern imperial eagle: Females are up to 40% heavier in body mass than males, despite being the same species.
Indian yellow-nosed albatross: They produce a stomach oil made of wax esters and triglycerides that they use against predators and as an energy-rich food source for chicks and during their long flights.
Oriental stork: In May 2007, a wild hatchling was reported in Japan for the first time in 40 years.
Pallas's fish eagle: Pallas's fish eagles have been recorded lifting and flying with carp weighing 6.5 kg (14 lb), which is roughly twice the weight of the eagle itself.
Philippine eagle: The Philippine eagle has a tarsus (lower leg bone) that ties as the longest of any eagle, measuring about the same length as that of the much smaller New Guinea eagle.
White stork: White storks avoid crossing the Mediterranean Sea during migration by detouring via the Levant or the Strait of Gibraltar because air thermals do not form over water.
Lesser yellow-headed vulture: The lesser yellow-headed vulture lacks a syrinx and can therefore only produce grunts or low hisses, unable to make any other sounds.
Black-necked crane: Black-necked cranes can distinguish between people wearing traditional dress and others, and are especially wary of those not in traditional clothing.
Black crowned crane: Black crowned cranes have a long hind toe that allows them to grasp onto perches and are known to roost in trees.
Blue crane: The blue crane aggressively attacks innocent, non-predatory animals such as antelope, cattle, tortoises, plovers, and sparrows when protecting its nesting sites, and has even torn clothes and drawn blood from humans who approached too closely.
Great-billed heron: The great-billed heron weighs up to only 2.6 kg (5.7 lb) despite standing 115 cm (45 inches) tall.
Harpy eagle: Female harpy eagles have talons measuring up to 12.3 cm (4.8 in) long, which are the largest talons of any living eagle.
White-naped crane: The eastern breeding white-naped cranes migrate more south through Korea while the western breeding cranes migrate through China to Poyang Lake, with Mongolia holding at least 50% of the entire white-naped crane population.
Blakiston's fish owl: Blakiston's fish owl has a wingspan that can reach approximately 200 cm (6 ft 7 in), making it larger than any known eagle-owl.
Great blue heron: The great blue heron is sometimes erroneously referred to as a "crane," when herons and cranes are actually quite different birds that can be distinguished in flight by the fact that cranes hold their necks straight while herons bend theirs into an S shape.
Hooded vulture: 45% of students at Ghanaian university campuses are defecated on by hooded vultures at least once a month.
Black vulture: Black vultures lack a syrinx, the vocal organ of birds, so they can only make grunts or low hisses.
Northern giant petrel: Northern giant petrels will kill other seabirds as large as an albatross.
Southern giant petrel: The southern giant petrel will drown other seabirds, including adult albatrosses, by holding them underwater.
Black swan: Black swans will abandon their nests, eggs, and cygnets if dry conditions return before the young have been raised.
Bateleur: It may take as long as 7 to 8 years to attain full maturity, perhaps the longest stretch to maturity of any raptor.
Short-toed snake eagle: Sometimes they become entangled with larger snakes and battle on the ground.
Gurney's eagle: The species is named after a British banker and amateur ornithologist rather than a naturalist or professional scientist.
Swan goose: The swan goose has a completely black bill, which is unique among its genus, while its legs and feet are orange like most of its relatives.
Eurasian eagle-owl: The Eurasian eagle-owl has the smallest wings proportionally relative to body weight of any European owl, with a wing loading of 0.72 g/cm2.
Black-chested snake eagle: Communal roosts of up to 200 black-chested snake eagles have been recorded in the non-breeding period.
Brown snake eagle: The brown snake eagle has been recorded hunting adult black mambas measuring up to at least 2.8 meters (9 feet 2 inches) long.
Madagascar fish eagle: The Madagascar fish eagle exhibits polyandrous breeding behavior where a single female mates with two males, which shows higher reproductive success compared to monogamous pairs.
Northern gannet: The northern gannet can dive into the sea at speeds up to 60 mph to catch fish.
Tawny eagle: The tawny eagle is the national animal of Ghana.
Hooded crane: In December 2011, a hooded crane was seen overwintering at the Hiwassee Refuge in southeastern Tennessee, well outside its normal range.
Horned screamer: The horned screamer has a long spiny structure projecting from its crown that is not made of feathers but is a cornified structure loosely attached to the skull that grows continuously while often breaking at its tip.
Canada goose: An exceptionally large male Canada goose weighed 10.9 kg (24 lb) with a wingspan of 2.24 m (7 ft 4 in), making it the largest wild goose ever recorded of any species.
Egyptian vulture: Egyptian vultures use twigs to roll up wool for use in their nests, demonstrating a second form of tool use beyond using pebbles to break eggs.
Demoiselle crane: The demoiselle crane is claimed to have inspired the first human-composed verse in Hindu tradition, when the poet Valmiki was moved to curse a hunter in meter after witnessing a male crane being killed by a hunter.
Greater spotted eagle: The oldest chick in a greater spotted eagle nest is much larger than its younger siblings and will often attack and kill them.
Great black-backed gull: Great black-backed gulls take at least four years to reach maturity, which is somewhat slower development than other large gulls.
Red kite: There is a rare white leucistic form of red kites that accounts for approximately 1% of hatchlings in the Welsh population but confers a disadvantage in survival.
Crowned eagle: There is at least one credible report of an attack, presumably a case of attempted predation, on a 19.6 kg human child.
Greater flamingo: The oldest known greater flamingo lived to at least 83 years old at the Adelaide Zoo in Australia.
Beaudouin's snake eagle: The total population is thought to be between 3,500 and 15,000 individuals.
Black-chested buzzard-eagle: Fossils of the black-chested buzzard-eagle were found in Cuba during the Pleistocene epoch, despite the species now living only in South America.
Taiga bean goose: The taiga and tundra bean goose diverged about 2.5 million years ago but established secondary contact around 60,000 years ago, resulting in extensive gene flow between them.
Pallas's gull: Pallas's gull takes four years to reach maturity.
Osprey: The osprey and owls are the only raptors whose outer toes are reversible, allowing them to grasp their prey with two toes in front and two behind.
Glaucous gull: Glaucous gulls on Wrangel Island averaged 2.32 kg in males and 2.1 kg in females, which would make them the heaviest gull and shorebird in the world.
Yellow-billed stork: A yellow-billed stork nestling increases its body weight from 50 grams to 600 grams during the first ten days of its life.
Greylag goose: The greylag goose was domesticated at least 3,000 years ago in Ancient Egypt and is the ancestor of most breeds of domestic goose.
Black-and-chestnut eagle: The talons of this species are very large and formidable, averaging nearly as large as those of a much bigger eagle such as a golden eagle.
White-necked heron: Young white-necked herons may knock smaller siblings out of the nest in competition for food.
American flamingo: Hurricanes have been known to drive flocks of flamingos north, leading to rare sightings in Kentucky, New York, Ohio, Pennsylvania and Virginia.
Crested serpent eagle: Some subspecies of crested serpent eagles weigh nearly three times more than others, with the heaviest individuals reaching approximately 2,300 grams while the smallest subspecies weigh around 420 grams.
Masked booby: The second chick born to masked boobies generally does not survive and is killed by its elder sibling.
Japanese cormorant: Japanese cormorants have been traditionally trained by fishermen in Japan to catch fish for them in a practice called ukai.
Black kite: Black kites in northern Australia deliberately pick up and drop burning twigs to start fires that flush out prey.
Verreaux's eagle-owl: Verreaux's eagle-owl has bright pink eyelids, a unique feature not shared with any other owl species.
Upland buzzard: The upland buzzard was not documented nesting in the Indian subcontinent until 1998, despite having a large range across Central and East Asia.
Pink-footed goose: Numbers wintering in Ireland and Great Britain have risen almost tenfold from 30,000 in 1950 to 292,000 in October 2004.
Pel's fishing owl: Pel's fishing owl has spiky scales on the bottoms of their feet to help them grip slippery fish, similar to diurnal raptors that specialize in fish.
Black stork: South Korea designated the black stork as natural monument 200 on 31 May 1968.
Wood stork: The wood stork can have up to 25 nests in a single tree.
Common raven: Common ravens can live more than 23 years in the wild.
Bar-headed goose: Bar-headed geese have a single amino acid point mutation in their hemoglobin that gives their blood higher oxygen affinity than low-altitude geese, allowing them to extract oxygen more efficiently from the thin air at high altitudes.
Palm-nut vulture: The palm-nut vulture feeds mainly on fruit husks and palm fruits rather than meat, which makes up over 60% of the adult bird's diet.
Yellow-footed gull: The yellow-footed gull is tied with the slaty-backed gull for the world's fourth-largest gull species.
Black-breasted buzzard: The black-breasted buzzard uses stones to crack open the eggs of large ground-nesting birds like emus, either by dropping or throwing the stones at the eggs.
Snow goose: Snow geese can be heard from more than a mile away because they are very vocal.
Pacific gull: Pacific gulls have salt glands that secrete salty water through their nostrils.
Crested eagle: A crested eagle was observed feeding a juvenile harpy eagle while the adult harpy eagles were away, and even brought nesting material to the harpy eagle's nest.
Iceland gull: The Iceland gull does not breed in Iceland, only wintering there.
Magpie goose: Some male magpie geese mate with two females, and all three parents raise the young together, which increases chick survival when predation is high.
Yellow-billed loon: In March 2024, a young yellow-billed loon was found resting in the Fountains of Bellagio on the Las Vegas Strip after being blown off course by strong winds.
Great egret: In 2012, a pair of great egrets was observed nesting in the UK for the first time, and by 2017, seven nests in Somerset had fledged 17 young.
Egyptian goose: Captive Egyptian geese have been recorded reaching an age of 35 years, more than double their typical wild lifespan of up to 15 years.
Brown skua: Brown skuas can identify individual human beings, possibly indicating high cognitive abilities.
Ferruginous hawk: The ferruginous hawk has the largest and most robust feet of any of the world's Buteos, with a grasp measuring 94 to 125 mm.
Crested honey buzzard: The crested honey buzzard has a groove in its tongue specifically for extracting wasp grubs from their comb cells.
Slaty-backed gull: On 3 November 2012, an individual was spotted in Finland, which was only the fourth sighting of this species in Europe.
White-chinned petrel: The white-chinned petrel produces a stomach oil made up of wax esters and triglycerides that it can spray out of its mouth as a defense against predators and as food for its chicks.
Caspian gull: During the breeding season, Caspian gulls fly some distance into the steppes to hunt for rodents such as ground squirrels.
African hawk-eagle: A 2024 study showed that the African hawk-eagle is experiencing a severe population decline of approximately 91%, despite being listed as Least Concern by the IUCN.
Greater white-fronted goose: The Greenland white-fronted goose has an unusually long period of parental care and association that may last several years and can include grandparenting, possibly unique among the Anseriformes.
Great cormorant: Great cormorants have fully webbed feet between all four toes, unlike most birds which only have webbing between three toes.
Armenian gull: The Armenian gull bears a remarkable resemblance to the California gull of North America despite their ranges not overlapping.
Papuan eagle: The Papuan eagle is capable of chasing down prey on the forest floor, making it unique amongst eagles as a surprisingly fast and agile terrestrial avian predator.
Glaucous-winged gull: A glaucous-winged gull banded as a chick in British Columbia lived for more than 37 years.
Square-tailed kite: Square-tailed kites have a tail that is about half the length of their entire body.
Great grey owl: Much of the great grey owl's large size is deceptive because its fluffy feathers, large head, and longest tail of any extant owl obscure a body lighter than that of most other large owls.
Westland petrel: Fledglings may not return to their breeding colony for up to 10 years after leaving the nesting sites.
Western gull: Western gulls have been observed directly pilfering milk from the elephant seal's teat.
Great skua: Great skuas have been observed hunting Leach's storm petrels at night using a remarkable strategy unusual for seabirds.
Thayer's gull: The American Ornithologists' Union classified Thayer's gull as a separate species in 1973, but then reclassified it as a subspecies of Iceland gull in 2017, reversing their earlier decision.
South polar skua: The south polar skua has been seen at the actual Geographic South Pole.
Kelp gull: Kelp gulls have been observed feeding on living right whales by pecking down centimeters into their skin and blubber, often leaving wounds half a meter in diameter.
Olrog's gull: Olrog's gull is named after a Swedish-Argentine biologist, not after a place or person from its native range.
Purple heron: The purple heron's long toes allow it to walk on floating vegetation and over bushes without gripping the branches.
Rough-legged buzzard: Some rough-legged buzzards may be able to see vole scent marks that are only visible in the ultraviolet range, allowing them to locate prey.
Jackal buzzard: The female jackal buzzard can weigh up to 1,700 grams (3.7 pounds), making it one of the more massive Buteo species in the world.
Augur buzzard: About 10% of augur buzzards are melanistic (all black), but this proportion rises to as much as 50% in some forested areas with high rainfall.
Changeable hawk-eagle: The changeable hawk-eagle was first described in 1676 from a live bird kept in the Royal Aviary in St James's Park, London.
European herring gull: European herring gulls are capable of seeing ultraviolet light.
Great horned owl: The great horned owl remains one of the few regular predators of skunk.
Chilean skua: Chilean skuas defend their nests by discharging excrement onto perceived threats from above.
Spotted harrier: Rabbit calicivirus disease caused a 65-85% decline in rabbit numbers in arid and semi-arid zones, which significantly changed the spotted harrier's diet from being heavily dependent on rabbits to relying more on native prey.
Lesser black-backed gull: The lesser black-backed gull takes four years to reach maturity.
Caspian tern: In winter, the Caspian tern retains its black cap unlike many other tern species.
Ruddy shelduck: A feral population of ruddy shelducks in Moscow increased from about 1 bird in the 1990s to 3,300 by 2025, originating from escapees of the Moscow Zoo after the policy of clipping their wings was stopped in 1948.
Whistling kite: Spotted bowerbirds regularly mimic the calls of whistling kites, with the frequency of mimicry increasing as the breeding season progresses.
Bank cormorant: The world population of bank cormorants is probably now around only 4,000 birds.
Audouin's gull: In the late 1960s, this species had a population of only 1,000 pairs, making it one of the world's rarest gulls.
Swallow-tailed gull: The swallow-tailed gull is the only fully nocturnal gull and seabird in the world.
Swamp harrier: Swamp harriers are the only bird of prey used for falconry in New Zealand.
California gull: The California gull is the state bird of Utah because it saved Mormon settlers from a plague of Mormon crickets, an event commemorated as the "Miracle of the Gulls."
Savanna hawk: There are reports of savanna hawks in California, from 1973 in Alameda County and from 1974 in San Diego County.
Yellow-legged gull: During Italy's 2020 lockdown, yellow-legged gulls in Rome shifted from scavenging on rubbish to hunting prey as large as rats and rock doves.
Eurasian spoonbill: The Eurasian spoonbill was extirpated from the United Kingdom around 1668 but has returned to breed there in recent decades, with the first successful breeding occurring in 1998.
Eastern buzzard: Some scientists treat the eastern buzzard as a distinct species while others treat it as one or three subspecies of the common buzzard, reflecting ongoing scientific disagreement about its classification.
Upland goose: Charles Darwin noted in On the Origin of Species that the upland goose has webbing between its toes that is "rudimentary in function, though not in structure," which he concluded was a vestigial anatomical feature.
Rufous-tailed hawk: Charles Darwin first recorded the rufous-tailed hawk in Patagonia during his voyage on the Beagle.
Zone-tailed hawk: The zone-tailed hawk resembles and may benefit from blending into groups of turkey vultures to hunt prey.
Swallow-tailed kite: The swallow-tailed kite has declined by roughly 80% in the United States, shrinking from historic ranges covering most of the Southern states and much of the Midwest to just coastal regions of the southeastern and southwestern US.
Swainson's hawk: The Swainson's hawk is the second longest migrant of any North American raptor, with migrations lasting at least two months and covering distances up to 7,100 miles between North American breeding grounds and South American wintering grounds.
Eurasian bittern: The Eurasian bittern produces white dusty powder from specialist down feathers on its breast to help remove slime from its head and neck after feeding on eels.
Crested caracara: Crested caracaras are the second-largest species of falcon in the world by mean body mass, second only to the gyrfalcon.
European honey buzzard: It is the only known predator of the invasive Asian hornet.
Scopoli's shearwater: After his death, Diomedes' distraught friends were turned into white seabirds, which is the mythological origin of the species name "diomedea."
Andaman serpent eagle: Juvenile Andaman serpent eagles have a white head, unlike the dark brown adults.
Powerful owl: Unlike most owl species, the male powerful owl is larger and stronger than the female.
Lesser white-fronted goose: Lesser white-fronted geese are not much bigger than a mallard, measuring only 53–66 cm in length.
Black-tailed gull: Over 40,000 black-tailed gulls nest and raise their young each summer in the grounds of a shrine in Japan that has protected them for over 700 years.
Madagascar harrier-hawk: The Madagascar harrier-hawk possesses an intertarsal joint that allows their legs to flex backwards and forwards, enabling them to probe into holes in tree trunks and rock crevices to extract prey from normally inaccessible places.
Variable hawk: At least 27 distinct adult plumages are known in this species, possibly the most of any raptor.
Common buzzard: Some common buzzards migrate as far as South Africa for the northern winter.
Mountain serpent eagle: The mountain serpent eagle can be found at altitudes up to 4,100 metres (13,500 feet), which is higher than many commercial aircraft fly.
Southern banded snake eagle: The southern banded snake eagle tears up large snakes into bite-size pieces before feeding, but swallows smaller snakes whole, head-first.
Mantled hawk: When threatened by humans, the mantled hawk defends its territory by regurgitating stomach contents at intruders.
Red goshawk: The female red goshawk weighs nearly twice as much as the male, with females weighing 1100–1400 g compared to males weighing 635 g.
Grey petrel: Grey petrels are the most commonly caught bycatch by longline fisheries in New Zealand waters, with an estimate of 45,000 birds caught in the last 20 years.
Cape eagle-owl: Cape eagle-owls can prey on hyraxes, which are heavier than the birds themselves.
Sulawesi hawk-eagle: Young Sulawesi hawk-eagles have white head and underparts, which is strikingly different from the rufous-brown and dark plumage of adults.
Réunion harrier: 93% of Réunion harriers tested between 1999 and 2016 had rodenticide residue in their livers.
Long-tailed honey buzzard: The Long-tailed honey buzzard follows wasps to their nests and pulls out larvae with their feet.
African darter: The African darter's feathers are not waterproof and do not contain oil, which actually enhances its diving capabilities but requires it to dry its feathers afterward by spreading its wings in the sun and wind.
Ayres's hawk-eagle: Ayres's hawk-eagle can sometimes overtake and hunt birds up to over twice their own size, including white-faced whistling ducks, francolins, guineafowls, and even other birds of prey.
Oriental darter: In some parts of northeastern India, darters were used by tribals to capture fish from streams by tying a ring around the bird's neck to prevent it from swallowing its prey.
Heermann's gull: In June 2018, one of the colony's main nesting sites, the Seaside McDonald's, was destroyed by a drunk driving crash.
Striated caracara: Striated caracaras have problem-solving abilities comparable to parrots.
Long-crested eagle: Long-crested eagles can contract trichomoniasis (canker) from doves they hunt, which causes damage to their crop, pharynx, and mouth that can eventually prevent them from eating.
Black-throated loon: The black-throated loon dives to catch food but goes no deeper than 5 metres (16 ft), yet most of its dives are successful.
Anhinga: Anhingas lack waterproof feathers and become saturated when they enter water, requiring them to spend time basking in the sun with their wings spread to dry off before they can fly.
Belcher's gull: Belcher's gull often pesters Guanay cormorants until they regurgitate their prey, which the gull then consumes.
Royal spoonbill: The royal spoonbill's bill end is broader and works like a pair of tongs, whereas the yellow-billed spoonbill's narrower bill acts like forceps.
Javan hawk-eagle: Without conservation, the Javan hawk-eagle was predicted to go extinct by 2025.
Australian pied cormorant: Cormorants lack adequate waterproofing on their feathers, which may actually help them spend longer underwater due to decreased buoyancy.
Wild turkey: Wild turkeys can fly up to 400 meters despite weighing up to 16.85 kilograms, making them among the heaviest flying birds in the world.
Emperor goose: The emperor goose's diet consists mostly of animals rather than vegetation like other geese, which causes its flesh to have a strong flavor.
Little eagle: The little eagle's diet changed dramatically after rabbits and foxes were introduced to Australia, with rabbits becoming its main food source until the calicivirus was released and decreased the rabbit population by 65-85%.
Pomarine jaeger: The pomarine jaeger's genus name Stercorarius is Latin meaning "of dung" because the food disgorged by other birds when pursued by skuas was once thought to be excrement.
African sacred ibis: Egyptian temples buried a few dozen thousand sacred ibises per year as votive offerings to the god Thoth.
Barred hawk: Barred hawks obtain 48% of their diet from La Bonita caecilians, which are large subterranean worm-like amphibians that can exceed 62 cm in length.
Royal tern: Royal terns feed on hatchling sea turtles.
Black petrel: A fledged black petrel has only a 1 in 20 chance of reaching breeding age and must breed 20 times successfully just to replace the current population.
Mountain caracara: The feathers of the mountain caracara were used to decorate the crown of the Sapa Inca.
Australian white ibis: The Australian white ibis is not a feral species introduced to Australia by people and does not come from Egypt, contrary to urban myth.
Brahminy kite: As many as 600 brahminy kites have been seen roosting together at a single location.
Barred honey buzzard: The barred honey buzzard can inhabit forests at elevations up to 1800 meters.
Pied harrier: Adult females weigh 390–455g while adult males weigh only 265–325g, making females significantly heavier than males.
Reddish egret: In Florida, reddish egrets may be eaten by invasive Burmese pythons, reticulated pythons, boa constrictors, and anacondas.
Southern fulmar: The southern fulmar ejects a foul-smelling oil, which is the origin of its genus name meaning "foul-mew" or "foul-gull."
White-throated caracara: As of early 2023, there were only two recordings of white-throated caracara vocalizations in each of the two major bird sound libraries (xeno-canto and the Cornell Lab of Ornithology's Macaulay Library).
Red-faced cormorant: The red-faced cormorant was originally placed in the genus Pelecanus (pelicans) when it was first formally described in 1789.
Western marsh harrier: Male western marsh harriers often pair with two and occasionally three females during breeding season.
Carunculated caracara: As of early 2023, the Cornell Lab of Ornithology's Macaulay Library had only one recording of the carunculated caracara's flight calls.
Greater crested tern: The greater crested tern has learned to follow fishing boats to catch jettisoned bycatch and nests on unusual sites such as roofs of buildings and artificial islands in salt pans and sewage works.
Ring-billed gull: The oldest ring-billed gull on record was observed in Cleveland in 2021 at the age of 28 years.
American bittern: The male American bittern produces its loud, booming call by gradually inflating its esophagus with air and then making a gulping sound in the syrinx.
Rufous owl: Rufous owl nests are sometimes built as high as thirty meters above the ground to protect them from ground-dwelling predators.
Philippine hawk-eagle: A nesting pair of Philippine hawk-eagles was livestreamed daily on Facebook during a 2020 study in Rizal.
Ornate hawk-eagle: Fossil hawk-eagle species such as Spizaetus willetti may have grown to similar sizes as the modern harpy eagle, considerably larger than any living hawk-eagle today.
Common shelduck: Moulting flocks of common shelduck can reach 100,000 birds on the Wadden Sea, with most pairs leaving their partially grown young in a crèche supervised by just one or two adults.
Sooty gull: The sooty gull feeds on newly hatched turtles and the eggs and chicks of other sea birds.
Australasian bittern: The Australasian bittern's "booming" call can be heard up to 4 kilometres away, despite the bird being so cryptic and difficult to spot that it is rarely seen.
Saker falcon: The saker falcon is the second fastest bird in level flight capable of reaching 150 km/h, and along with the peregrine falcon and golden eagle, can execute high-speed dives approaching 300 km/h.
Ivory gull: There is a high risk that the ivory gull could become the first vertebrate to become extinct due to human-induced global warming.
Booted eagle: The paler plumage color in booted eagles is dominant over the dark color morph, which follows a Mendelian inheritance pattern.
Common gull: The common gull takes three years (up to four in the Kamchatka subspecies) to reach maturity.
Kerguelen shag: The Kerguelen shag forms linear flocks of several hundred birds from May to October that dive and surface sequentially down the line.
Little bustard: The male little bustard has a distinctive "raspberry-blowing" call.
Black-faced spoonbill: In the winter of 2002/2003, 73 black-faced spoonbills died from avian botulism.
Harris's hawk: Harris's hawks are known to hunt cooperatively in packs, a behavior unique among birds of prey, with no other raptor species hunting in groups as routinely as this species.
Straw-necked ibis: Straw-necked ibises have learned to eat cane toads by flicking them about until they release their toxin, then washing them in a creek to avoid being poisoned.
Hen harrier: Hen harriers are one of the few raptorial birds known to practice polygyny, with up to five females known to mate with one male in a season.
Red-throated caracara: The red-throated caracara mainly feeds on the larvae of bees and wasps, making it unique among caracaras in this dietary specialization.
Black harrier: Black harriers travel almost twice as fast during their summer post-breeding migration as during their winter/spring pre-breeding migration, which is unusual since many other species show the opposite pattern.
Mountain buzzard: In Uganda, mountain buzzards have been recorded hunting bats at caves.
Forest buzzard: When food is scarce, the younger chick in a forest buzzard nest will starve because its older sibling prevents it from eating.
Cape cormorant: Cape cormorants can dive to depths of up to 150 meters deep while foraging for fish.
Barred owl: Barred owls have expanded their range to the west coast of North America where they are now considered invasive and are encroaching on the range of the threatened spotted owl, prompting biologists to recommend culling operations.
White-headed petrel: White-headed petrels have green glands that are prominent.
Montagu's harrier: Up to 70% of the population of Montagu's harrier breeds in artificial habitats such as agricultural farmlands in western Europe.
Pallid harrier: In 2017, a pair of pallid harriers nested in a barley field in the Netherlands and raised four chicks, marking the first recorded breeding of the species in the country.
Indian skimmer: The Indian skimmer's lower mandible is longer than its upper mandible and is dragged through the water while flying to catch fish.
Cassin's hawk-eagle: Cassin's hawk-eagle engages in a visual mimicry system with the Congo serpent eagle, where the two predatory species show remarkable similarity in plumage, body size, and proportions despite differing greatly in diet.
Barnacle goose: Goslings jump off cliffs up to 200-300 meters high and survive the fall because their small size, feathery down, and light weight protect them from injury when they hit the rocks below.
Bat hawk: The bat hawk has the largest gape of any raptor relative to body size, which is more similar to insectivorous birds like swallows and swifts than to other birds of prey.
Flesh-footed shearwater: The flesh-footed shearwater ingests significant quantities of plastic, which it mistakes for food floating on the ocean surface.
White hawk: The white hawk has been documented feeding on fish, though this behavior is rare enough that one researcher in Suriname finding fish remains in a white hawk's stomach was noteworthy.
Eurasian whimbrel: The Eurasian whimbrel has a white rump while the Hudsonian whimbrel has a drab brown rump with dark streaking, which is the most significant difference between the two species.
Dolphin gull: Dolphin gulls take advantage of human disturbance of nesting seabirds to raid their abandoned nests, and excluding humans from cormorant nesting areas increases the cormorants' reproductive success.
Snail kite: Snail kites in Florida quickly evolved to be 12% bigger to adapt to eating invasive South American snails that were five times larger than the native species.
Northern fulmar: Northern fulmars can spray a foul-smelling stomach oil from their mouths as a defense mechanism that will mat the plumage of avian predators and can lead to their death.
Spotted eagle-owl: Sometimes a male spotted eagle-owl will tear the head off a mouse and bring only the body to the female to feed to the young or herself if the eggs have not yet hatched.
Yellow-crowned night heron: The yellow-crowned night heron can be found nesting on rooftops and driveways in wooded neighborhoods, living in close proximity to humans.
Cape Verde shearwater: Approximately 5,000 Cape Verde shearwater chicks are harvested from their nests on Branco and Raso islands each year by fishermen for food.
Western capercaillie: The largest western capercaillie specimen recorded in captivity weighed 7.2 kg (15 lb 14 oz).
African skimmer: The African skimmer's lower mandible is much longer than the upper mandible and is flattened sideways like scissor blades.
Parasitic jaeger: The genus name Stercorarius is Latin meaning "of dung" because the food disgorged by other birds when pursued by skuas was once thought to be excrement.
White-faced heron: The white-faced heron will boldly raid suburban fish ponds despite promptly departing when disturbed.
Socotra cormorant: Roosting flocks of Socotra cormorants have been reported with up to 250,000 birds.
Black-crowned night heron: Black-crowned night herons are among the seven heron species observed to engage in bait fishing, luring or distracting fish by tossing edible or inedible buoyant objects into water—a rare example of tool use among birds.
Nankeen night heron: The nankeen night heron subspecies that was endemic to the Bonin Islands, Japan, has been extinct since the late 1800s.
Grey-faced buzzard: Grey-faced buzzards utilize the world's only oceanic flyway for raptor migration.
Black falcon: The black falcon's nestling period lasts 5.5 to 7 weeks, yet it can live at least 12 years in the wild and up to 20 years in captivity.
Laughing gull: Laughing gulls take three years to reach adult plumage.
Campbell shag: Campbell Island was officially declared "rat-free" in 2003 after 200 years of Norway rats that were considered the densest population of rats in the world.
Antarctic petrel: The largest single Antarctic petrel colony ever observed, at the Mühlig-Hofmann Mountains, was estimated to contain about one million birds.
Flying steamer duck: Some individual male flying steamer ducks are incapable of flight because they are too large and heavy.
Juan Fernández petrel: The Juan Fernández petrel forages as far north as the Hawaiian Islands during the non-breeding season, despite nesting only in the Juan Fernández Archipelago off the coast of Chile.
Galápagos petrel: Galápagos petrels can find their nest burrow within the colony in the dark using their sense of smell.
Atlantic petrel: House mice on Gough Island have rapidly evolved to be about twice the size of their mainland counterparts.
Madagascar serpent eagle: The scientific name Eutriorchis astur literally means "having three testicles," based on an erroneous anatomical observation that arose from mistaking a bird's adrenal gland for a testicle.
Red-throated loon: The red-throated loon's lineage diverged from other loon species approximately 21.4 million years ago during the Miocene period.
Far Eastern curlew: The species was named madagascariensis after Madagascar by Brisson, but has never actually been recorded in Madagascar and was originally found in Makassar on Sulawesi, Indonesia.
Eastern chanting goshawk: The eastern chanting goshawk is usually seen in large groups of up to 16 and will always hunt in a pack and ration their finds amongst each other.
Greater sooty owl: Greater sooty owls appear to be the heaviest living species in the barn owl family, though the Tasmanian subspecies of the Australian masked owl is larger still.
Red-billed tropicbird: Male red-billed tropicbirds have tail streamers that are about two times their body length.
Grey-headed gull: Recent genetic evidence suggests that the African and South American populations of grey-headed gulls may actually be two separate species rather than subspecies of the same species.
Red-naped ibis: In some towns and cities, red-naped ibises have recently learned to build nests on cell phone towers and electricity pylons instead of trees, a behavior that was not observed in studies from the 1990s.
Slender-billed gull: A vagrant slender-billed gull was reportedly seen on Antigua in the Caribbean on April 24, 1976, far from its normal range around the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean.
Short-eared owl: The short-eared owl can lay up to a dozen eggs in a single clutch during years when voles are abundant, compared to the typical 4 to 7 eggs.
Peregrine falcon: The malar stripe (black patch below the falcon's eyes) is larger in areas that receive more sunlight because it reduces glare and improves the falcon's vision.
Magenta petrel: The magenta petrel was believed to be extinct for 111 years before being rediscovered alive in 1978.
Madagascar buzzard: Once the eggs hatch, the older sibling usually kills the younger and only very rarely are two chicks raised to fledging.
Red-shouldered hawk: Steller's jays and blue jays sometimes habitually imitate the call of the red-shouldered hawk and are known to be difficult to distinguish on voice alone.
Brown falcon: Brown falcons and other Australian firehawks have been said to pick up sticks with flames and drop them to spread fires and prey on escaping animals.
Buller's shearwater: A vagrant Buller's shearwater was once recorded in the Atlantic Ocean offshore New Jersey, despite the species being a Pacific species.
Sooty shearwater: Sooty shearwaters can travel 74,000 km in a year, averaging more than 500 km per day.
Black-capped petrel: The species name "diablotín" means "little devil" because locals believed the birds' night-time habits and odd-sounding mating calls were evidence of evil spirits in the dark.
Brant (goose): In County Kerry in Ireland, until relatively recently, Catholics could eat brant geese on Fridays because the bird counted as fish.
Providence petrel: An estimated 1 million providence petrels were harvested for food and the species was completely exterminated on Norfolk Island by 1800.
Little blue heron: Juvenile little blue herons are entirely white and resemble snowy egrets, only beginning to lose their white feathers in their second year of life.
Lanner falcon: Edward I of England, who reigned from 1272-1307, owned at least one lanner falcon.
Caspian snowcock: The Caspian snowcock's desolate whistling song is vaguely like a Eurasian curlew, which is a wading bird from a completely different order.
Sandwich tern: Sandwich terns breeding in Great Britain migrate over 10,000 kilometers to the west coast of Africa, while those breeding on the Caspian Sea coasts migrate to the Indian Subcontinent.
Long-tailed jaeger: Up to 29 cm (11 in) of the long-tailed jaeger's 38–58 cm length can be made up by its tail, with tail streamers reaching 15 cm (5.9 in) in summer adults.
Little black cormorant: The little black cormorant's eggs are covered with a thin layer of lime, giving them a matte white coated appearance, but become increasingly stained with faeces over the breeding season.
Red-tailed tropicbird: The red-tailed tropicbird's tail streamers are about twice its body length.
Gray-headed kite: Immature gray-headed kites have three distinct color morphs that closely resemble three different species of hawk-eagles, demonstrating remarkable mimicry sophistication.
Wallace's hawk-eagle: Wallace's Hawk-Eagle is among the smallest eagles in the world, weighing only 500–610 grams (about the size of a peregrine falcon).
Black-faced cormorant: The black-faced cormorant's closest relative, the pied cormorant, is slightly larger, yet the black-faced cormorant is described as "one of the largest cormorants found in south-western Australia."
Tawny owl: The tawny owl's retina is no more sensitive than a human's, despite claims that owls can see 10 to 100 times better than humans in low-light conditions.
Grasshopper buzzard: Grasshopper buzzards can occur at densities of up to 3.3 nests per square kilometre with distances between nests of less than 100 metres, despite having overlapping home ranges.
Hawaiian petrel: Hawaiian petrels travel as far as Alaska and Japan during their two-week-long feeding trips while breeding in Hawaii.
Great-winged petrel: The great-winged petrel catches its prey mostly at night by dipping and surface-seizing.
Common eider: Common eiders in Switzerland have colonized inland lakes since 1988, which is unusual because they are normally a strictly marine species.
Rufous crab hawk: The rufous crab hawk's diet is mostly, and possibly entirely, crabs.
Hooded crow: The hooded crow and carrion crow are nearly genetically identical except for a small portion (<0.28%) of the genome that causes the difference in plumage coloration, yet they rarely interbreed due to assortative mating based on appearance.
Lesser flamingo: The lesser flamingo's pink color comes from blue-green algae they eat, not from the algae's color itself.
Laggar falcon: The laggar falcon's lineage arrived at its present range from eastern Africa by way of the Arabian Peninsula only 130,000-115,000 years ago during the Eemian interglacial.
Eurasian curlew: Sheep have been recorded preying on curlew eggs.
Plains-wanderer: The adult female plains-wanderer is substantially larger than the male, which is unusual among bird species where males are typically larger.
Black-headed gull: Black-headed gulls have a maximum recorded lifespan of at least 32.9 years in the wild.
Velvet scoter: The velvet scoter's eggs are covered with down when the female leaves the nest.
Lesser crested tern: Lesser crested terns in the Mediterranean breed in late summer to avoid predation by yellow-legged gulls that have finished breeding and departed by that time.
Pelagic cormorant: Pelagic cormorants have the highest flight costs of any bird due to their relatively short wings.
Red-footed booby: Coconut crabs hunt adult red-footed boobies by breaking their wings with powerful claws or dragging them into burrows.
Mediterranean gull: The Mediterranean gull bred in Scotland for the first time in 2023, despite having traditionally been restricted to the Black Sea and eastern Mediterranean.
Black-legged kittiwake: Black-legged kittiwakes are unique among gulls in having only three toes, with their hind toe either extremely reduced or completely absent.
White-necked hawk: As of late 2022, there were only four total recordings of the white-necked hawk's vocalizations in existence.
Barau's petrel: Young fledgling petrels mistake artificial lights for bioluminescent squid, causing an estimated 40% of each breeding season's fledglings to become disoriented and fail to reach the sea.
Short-tailed shearwater: When the chicks fledge they weigh around 900 g (2 lb), and may be heavier than their parents.
Black caracara: Black caracaras have been observed cleaning ectoparasites and insects from tapirs and capybaras, with the animals often soliciting this service by emitting calls and lying still on the ground.
Pacific reef heron: Pacific reef herons hunt by standing motionless with their wings open like an umbrella to reduce reflections and create shade that attracts fish.
Great stone-curlew: The great thick-knee lays only a single egg in a bare scrape on the open shingle.
Spotted shag: Red-billed gulls steal food from young spotted shags by standing on the nest edge and intimidating the chicks into regurgitating their meals.
Long-eared owl: Long-eared owls do not build their own nests but instead use nests built by other animals, particularly corvids.
Dark chanting goshawk: The dark chanting goshawk has been recorded following honey badgers, Southern ground-hornbills, dogs or people to catch small animals disturbed by their passage.
Madagascar cuckoo-hawk: The Madagascar cuckoo-hawk has a wingspan of 850 mm (33 inches) despite being only 300 mm (12 inches) in length.
Eleonora's falcon: Eleonora's falcons catch and imprison small birds alive in rock crevices, removing their flight feathers and feeding them to their young sometimes days later, which is unique among bird species.
Herald petrel: In May 2007, a Herald petrel that had been tagged on Raine Island in 1984 was observed off the coast of Mauritius in the Indian Ocean, revealing the species travels vast distances across oceanic regions.
Desertas petrel: Unlike many other seabird species that flee from hurricanes, the Desertas petrel follows the paths of hurricanes to feed on fish and squid brought to the surface by the increased ocean mixing.
Trindade petrel: The Trindade petrel was previously thought to be the same species as the Herald petrel until the two populations were determined to be distinct.
Gull-billed tern: The gull-billed tern does not plunge dive for fish like other terns, but instead feeds largely on insects taken in flight and hunts over wet fields and brushy areas to catch amphibians and small mammals.
White-tailed kite: White-tailed kites can eat insects while flying by transferring prey from their talons to their beaks mid-flight rather than landing on a perch to feed.
Hawaiian hawk: Hawaii has almost no native land mammals, with the Hawaiian hoary bat being the only one.
Silver gull: Silver gulls have twice been recorded in the United States, with both birds believed to have escaped from captivity.
Henderson petrel: Pacific rats eat approximately 25,000 Henderson petrel chicks per year on the island.
King eider: The oldest known king eider was a female that lived at least 18 years 11 months after being banded as an adult in Alaska in 1940.
Henst's goshawk: Henst's goshawk hunts lemurs ranging from 100g to 4 kg in size, making it an important predator in maintaining lemur population health by removing diseased and old individuals.
White-eyed buzzard: Fledgelings of the white-eyed buzzard are reddish brown, unlike most other downy raptor chicks, which tend to be white.
Murphy's petrel: Murphy's petrels have one of the greatest foraging ranges of any breeding seabird, with most reports occurring over 64 km offshore.
Rook (bird): Rooks have been trained to pick up litter in a theme park in France.
Common merganser: Common mergansers are known to form crèches, with single females having been observed with over 70 ducklings at one time.
Short-tailed hawk: In one study, a short-tailed hawk's hunting success rate was only around 11%, with only 12 successful hunts out of 107 attempts observed over 30 hours.
White-throated hawk: The white-throated hawk's diet includes birds as large as the Magellanic woodpecker, despite being a relatively small hawk itself at only 38 to 48 cm long.
Pacific baza: The Pacific baza is an omnivore that frequently eats fruit intentionally, and captive specimens in 1981 would not breed without lettuce as part of their diet.
Little egret: During courtship, the little egret's lores turn red and its yellow feet turn red.
Grey falcon: A grey falcon has been observed pursuing a bat while hunting.
Kermadec petrel: In 1908, the Kermadec petrel had an estimated population of approximately 500,000 individuals on Raoul Island, but no longer breeds there due to predation by cats and rats.
Franklin's gull: Franklin's gull takes three years to reach maturity.
White-tailed tropicbird: The white-tailed tropicbird's central tail feathers double its total length.
Fea's petrel: A drawing of a specimen from Captain Cook's first voyage in 1768 can be recognized as Fea's petrel, making it the first known recording of this species over 130 years before it was formally described.
Carrion crow: Carrion crows and hooded crows are genetically identical except for a small portion (<0.28%) of their genome that controls plumage coloration, yet they rarely interbreed due to assortative mating based on appearance alone.
Marsh owl: Marsh owls have feathered legs and toes covered with pale buffish plumes, leaving only the dark brown tips of their claws bare.
Hartlaub's gull: About one half of the total population of Hartlaub's gulls, currently estimated at 30,000 birds, lives within the Greater Cape Town area.
American black duck: The American black duck's diet consists of approximately 80% plant food and 20% animal food during breeding season, but this reverses to 85% animal food during winter.
Little cormorant: Little cormorants sometimes forage in water less than a metre deep and capture fish only 2-8 centimetres long, yet other birds including gulls and egrets attempt to steal their catch from them.
Tricolored heron: The tricolored heron is almost exclusively piscivorous, with studies showing its diet consists of 99.7% fish and prawns.
Sooty tern: Sooty terns lack oil in their feathers so they cannot float and must stay airborne for 3 to 10 years at a time, never landing except to breed.
Northern pintail: A northern pintail ringed in Labrador, Canada, was shot by a hunter in England just nine days later.
Rufous-legged owl: The juvenile rufous-legged owl has a tawny face, while the adult has a rusty facial disk.
Black-billed gull: In 2024, black-billed gulls nesting in Christchurch were deemed so much of a nuisance that locals wanted them removed, but nothing could be done because of their protected status.
African cuckoo-hawk: The African cuckoo-hawk has two breeding periods per year in Kenya, timed to follow the two rainy seasons.
Great Nicobar serpent eagle: The Great Nicobar serpent eagle is probably the smallest known eagle, weighing only about 450 grams (0.99 pounds).
Western barn owl: Melanistic barn owls occur with odds of 1 out of every 100,000 birds.
Glossy ibis: The first successful breeding of glossy ibis in Britain occurred in 2022 in Cambridgeshire, after the species had been absent as a regular breeding bird for centuries.
Soft-plumaged petrel: The soft-plumaged petrel has occurred as a vagrant in Norway and Jordan, despite breeding only in the Southern Hemisphere.
Red-legged kittiwake: Juveniles take three years to reach maturity.
Bermuda petrel: Spanish sailors would take up to 4,000 Bermuda petrels in a single night for food.
Common tern: Terns replace their primary wing feathers at least twice, occasionally three times in a year, which is unusual compared to most other bird species.
Great crested grebe: The Royal Society for the Protection of Birds was established to help protect the great crested grebe after it was hunted almost to extinction in the United Kingdom during the 19th century for its head plumes used to decorate ladies' hats and garments.
Mascarene petrel: Despite being critically endangered with an extremely small population, the Mascarene petrel has high genetic diversity.
Mallard: Female mallards can spontaneously undergo sex reversal and develop male plumage, often caused by damaged or nonfunctioning ovaries.
Japanese night heron: The Japanese night heron migrates in mixed flocks of more than 1,000 other herons and sometimes overshoots its destination to Indonesia and Palau instead of the Philippines.
Black-shouldered kite: Chicks can hunt for mice within a week of leaving the nest.
Hook-billed kite: The hook-billed kite's beak size and shape has adapted in different regions in response to the species of tree snail available to them.
Caucasian snowcock: The bird's poor flight ability means it can only glide or drift down hills and relies primarily on its legs to bounce between rocky slopes.
Little pied cormorant: Nearly 30% by weight of the little pied cormorant's diet consists of crustaceans, with some individuals consuming up to 80% crustaceans.
Mottled owl: The mottled owl shows the greatest degree of sexual dimorphism of any species of owl, with females being considerably larger than males.
Pacific black duck: The now-extinct Mariana mallard was probably originally derived from hybrids between the Pacific black duck and the mallard.
Long-tailed hawk: The long-tailed hawk's tail comprises about 56% of its total body length, tying it with the cinereous harrier as the raptor with the longest tail relative to its body size.
Snowy egret: The snowy egret's species name "thula" comes from a Mapudungun term for the black-necked swan, applied to this bird in error by a Chilean naturalist in 1782.
Ross's gull: A Ross's gull's pink coloring is diet-related and can be lost in winter in some individuals but not others.
American oystercatcher: American oystercatchers can sometimes be drowned when a shellfish clamps down on their bill and holds them in place until the tide comes in.
Red-breasted merganser: During courtship, females often chase or even attack the males that are displaying for their attention.
Red-crested pochard: The majority of red-crested pochards in the British Isles are the result of accidental and deliberate introductions rather than natural migration from the continent.
Sabine's gull: Sabine's gulls migrate up to 32,000–39,000 km per year, which is the longest migration of any gull.
Malayan night heron: People in Taiwan assisted an immature-adult pair of Malayan night herons by building a base for their poorly constructed nest.
Grey goshawk: The white morph of the grey goshawk and the closely related variable goshawk are the only birds of prey in the world to be entirely white.
Letter-winged kite: The letter-winged kite is the only nocturnal species within the order Accipitriformes.
Slender-billed curlew: The only known recording of the slender-billed curlew's call was made by French ornithologist Claude Chappuis and donated to Cornell University after his death in 2021.
Kagu: The kagu has only one-third as many red blood cells and three times more hemoglobin per red blood cell than is usual in birds.
Squacco heron: The squacco heron's appearance is dramatically transformed in flight, when it looks very white due to the colour of its wings, despite having a buff-brown back when perched.
Pintado petrel: Pintado petrels will spit stomach oil at competitors, including their own species, while feeding.
South American tern: An 1877 breeding colony in Argentina contained an estimated 135,000 nesting birds with 112,500 eggs packed so densely that it was difficult to walk between the nests without crushing eggs.
Pygmy cormorant: In Iran, the pygmy cormorant is commercialized for cooking.
Common scoter: In parts of nineteenth-century France, the Roman Catholic Church accepted common scoters as a substitute for fish during Friday fasts because they were considered to have a "fishy flavour."
Brown goshawk: Brown goshawks will occasionally chase small mammals on foot.
Snow petrel: The word "petrel" is derived from Peter the Apostle and the story of his walking on water, in reference to the bird's habit of appearing to run on the water to take off.
Slender-billed kite: Slender-billed kites remove the digestive tract from snails before eating them, and like snail kites, they hunt by holding the snail in their talon and using their hooked beak to remove the operculum and pull the body from the shell.
Wandering whistling duck: Their wings make a whistling noise during flight.
Broad-winged hawk: Broad-winged hawks rarely drink water and are able to survive solely with the water present in their prey.
Reed cormorant: The reed cormorant will occasionally eat small birds.
Yelkouan shearwater: On Le Levant Island, cats kill thousands of yelkouan shearwater birds each year and it is estimated that this may lead to local extinction in several decades.
Yellow-headed caracara: The yellow-headed caracara has been observed foraging for small invertebrates in the fur of brown-throated three-toed sloths.
Gray-lined hawk: The gray-lined hawk weighs only 475 grams on average, making it lighter than a pound despite being 46-61 centimeters long.
Bristle-thighed curlew: Bristle-thighed curlews are the only shorebirds known to use tools, breaking open other birds' eggs with rocks.
Tahiti petrel: The Tahiti petrel consumes deep-sea fish despite not diving, indicating it scavenges dead organisms or seizes prey at night when they migrate vertically in the water column.
Bonaparte's gull: Bonaparte's gulls are known to engage in kleptoparasitism, stealing earthworms from foraging dunlins and black-bellied plovers.
Philippine duck: Flocks of the Philippine duck have been observed with up to 2,000 individuals, possibly even larger before heavy hunting reduced their numbers.
Surf scoter: During their molt in late July through early August, surf scoters become completely flightless for about four weeks.
Bulwer's petrel: Bulwer's petrels return to the exact same nest site and mate with the same partner every breeding season.
Black-winged kite: Black-winged kites can raise multiple broods in a single year, unlike most birds of prey.
Phoenix petrel: The Phoenix petrel was formally described in 1789 based on a specimen that had belonged to the naturalist Joseph Banks, which was originally described as a "white-breasted petrel" in 1785.
Black-vented shearwater: Black-vented shearwaters are the best performing free-divers in their genus, capable of diving to depths of 52 meters and staying underwater for up to 88 seconds.
Newell's shearwater: Young birds of Newell's shearwater are attracted to the lights of urban areas at night and many die in collisions with power lines and buildings.
Laughing falcon: The laughing falcon mainly feeds on snakes, including venomous ones such as coral snakes.
Chinese pond heron: A Chinese pond heron was recorded in North America for the first time when a stray bird stopped over on Saint Paul Island, Alaska on August 4–9, 1997.
Indian pond heron: The Indian pond heron has been observed to pick up crumbs of bread and drop them on the water surface to bait fishes.
Plumed whistling duck: Rather than diving for food in bodies of water like other ducks, the plumed whistling duck feeds by cropping grass on land.
Eurasian oystercatcher: An adult oystercatcher ringed in England in 1982 was recaptured in 2025, making it at least 46 years old.
Brown noddy: When the brown noddy fledgling first leaves the nest after six to seven weeks, it can sometimes weigh more than its parents, although this extra weight is lost quickly once it starts to fly.
Southern lapwing: The southern lapwing is equipped with red bony extensions under its wings called spurs, which it uses to intimidate foes and fight birds of prey.
Greater scaup: Greater scaup can dive to depths of 0.5–6 meters, and exceptionally up to 10 meters underwater to find food.
Canvasback: Canvasback ducks have been spotted multiple times in England after crossing the Atlantic, with at least five confirmed sightings recorded.
Manx shearwater: A 50-year-old Manx shearwater has probably covered over a million kilometers on migration alone.
Zino's petrel: The Portuguese name "Freira" (nun) comes from inhabitants of Nun's Valley claiming that the nocturnal wailing of the petrels were the calls of suffering souls of nuns who had fled there from French pirates in 1566.
Fox kestrel: The fox kestrel sometimes hunts for flying insects that are escaping from grass fires.
Red-billed chough: The red-billed chough has been recorded at 7,950 metres (26,080 feet) altitude on Mount Everest.
Sooty falcon: Sooty falcons lay their eggs in mid-summer (July-August) and breed during the hottest months in arid deserts, which is remarkable and unlike most other falcons.
Eurasian stone-curlew: A Eurasian stone-curlew ringed as a nestling in Suffolk in 1990 was recaptured in the same location in 2012, making it at least 22 years and 4 months old.
Javan pond heron: The Javan pond heron's non-breeding plumage is virtually indistinguishable in the field from that of the Chinese and Indian pond herons.
Kerguelen petrel: The egg of the Kerguelen petrel is unusually round for the family Procellariidae.
Red-knobbed coot: The red-knobbed coot's white facial shield is only fully developed at about one year old, even though the bird's black plumbing develops at 3-4 months old.
Arctic tern: The Arctic tern travels approximately 2.4 million kilometers during its lifetime, equivalent to a roundtrip from Earth to the Moon more than three times.
Masked lapwing: The masked lapwing can damage its wing while striking at intruders during nesting season and become flightless while the wing heals.
Blackish oystercatcher: The blackish oystercatcher can rarely be found on sandy beaches hunting for mole crabs.
African wood owl: If there is a food shortage, siblicide occurs among the young owls in the nest.
Cinereous harrier: The cinereous harrier's tail comprises about 56% of its total body length, tying it with the long-tailed hawk as the raptor with the longest tail relative to its body size.
Redhead (bird): Redheads migrate in pairs that are formed through elaborate courtship rituals, but unpaired birds migrate together in "courting parties" of up to 25 individuals to find mates within the group.
Jouanin's petrel: Jouanin's petrel has been recorded breeding on Socotra, with a known breeding colony on Samhah island.
Townsend's shearwater: Feral cats introduced to Socorro in the early 1970s prey on shearwaters immediately after they land, taking advantage of a lack of understory vegetation that leaves the birds exposed.
Andean avocet: The Andean avocet is likely the heaviest species in the entire avocet and stilt bird family, with an average body mass of 361 grams.
Gundlach's hawk: The Cuban macaw, which Gundlach's hawk preyed upon, is now extinct.
Collared forest falcon: The collared forest falcon's diet ranges from prey as small as 20-gram frogs to adult turkeys weighing 2.7-3.2 kilograms.
Mississippi kite: Groups of up to 10,000 Mississippi kites may be observed migrating together at one time, such as at Fuerte Esperanza, Argentina.
Australian wood duck: Finsch's duck, an extinct flightless species from New Zealand that was formerly classified in its own genus, has been determined to belong to the same genus as the Australian wood duck.
Elegant tern: In May 2021, a drone crash at Bolsa Chica Ecological Reserve caused 2,500 elegant terns to abandon 1500 nests with thousands of eggs, resulting in catastrophic loss.
Bridled tern: The bridled tern's species name "anaethetus" means "senseless, stupid."
New Zealand falcon: The New Zealand falcon sometimes catches prey several times before killing it, as play or practice.
Lady Amherst's pheasant: Despite the male's showy and brightly colored appearance, these birds are very difficult to see in their natural habitat due to the dense, dark forests with thick undergrowth where they live.
White-cheeked tern: Colonies of white-cheeked terns can consist of a mixture of tern species rather than being composed of only their own species.
Pied avocet: Some male pied avocets have been observed attempting to mate with inanimate objects.
Little gull: The little gull is the smallest species of gull in the world, with a body length of only 24 to 28 cm (9.4 to 11.0 in).
Atlantic white tern: The white tern lays its egg on a bare thin branch in a small fork or depression without building any nest at all.
Bee hummingbird: The bee hummingbird beats its wings 80–200 times per second.
European robin: The European robin is the sole extant member of its genus Erithacus, as the Japanese robin and Ryukyu robin were moved to a different genus based on molecular evidence showing they were more similar to other Asian species.
House sparrow: The house sparrow is the most widely distributed wild bird in the world due to its intentional or accidental introductions to many regions including parts of Australasia, Africa, and the Americas.
Common kingfisher: The common kingfisher must eat around 60% of its body weight each day.
Atlantic puffin: The name "puffin" was originally applied to the fatty, salted meat of young Manx shearwaters, not to the bird we now call a puffin.
Resplendent quetzal: Their tail feathers can measure up to 100.5 cm (39.6 inches) long, exceeding the bird's body length of 36 to 40 cm.
Snowy owl: The global snowy owl population has declined from over 200,000 individuals to probably fewer than 100,000 in recent decades.
Toco toucan: The toco toucan's beak looks heavy but is relatively light because the inside is largely hollow.
Scarlet macaw: Scarlet macaws can live up to 75 or even 90 years in captivity.
Emperor penguin: Male emperor penguins fast for more than two months while incubating their eggs, losing around 12 kg (26 lb) during this time.
Shoebill: The shoebill's flapping rate is estimated at 150 flaps per minute, which is one of the slowest of any bird.
American robin: The American robin is the most abundant landbird in North America with 370 million individuals.
Northern cardinal: A captive northern cardinal lived to be 28.5 years old.
Blue jay: Blue jays have sometimes been found to have decapitated other birds.
Indian peafowl: Despite the length and size of the covert feathers, the peacock is still capable of flight.
Red-tailed hawk: The red-tailed hawk is one of three species colloquially known as the "chickenhawk," though it rarely preys on adult chickens.
Eurasian magpie: The Eurasian magpie's nidopallium is approximately the same relative size as the brains of chimpanzees, gorillas, orangutans and humans.
American crow: Crows have the same brain-weight-to-body ratio as humans.
Rock dove: The word "pigeon" comes from Ancient Greek meaning "diver" because of the bird's swimming motion in the air.
Barn swallow: Barn swallows have been recorded covering up to 11,660 km (7,250 miles) on their annual migration.
Pileated woodpecker: Pileated woodpeckers may abandon a nest and move to another site if any eggs have fallen out of the nest, which is a rare habit in birds.
Common ostrich: Unlike all other living birds, the common ostrich secretes urine separately from faeces.
